blocks.content: Spirit Airlines has ceased operations after a last-minute rescue deal collapsed, leaving thousands of travelers stranded and marking the largest airline bankruptcy in recent memory. Meanwhile, escalating U.S.-Iran military tensions have forced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz—a chokepoint handling roughly 20% of global oil shipments—sending shockwaves through energy markets and stoking fears of a broader economic fallout.

For investors, the dual shocks of airline insolvency and a potential oil crisis compound existing concerns about Federal Reserve policy divergence. Berkshire Hathaway's record $397 billion cash hoard underscores how even the savviest investors are sitting on the sidelines, signaling deep uncertainty about the near-term outlook.
• S&P 500: Slides 1.8% as energy costs spike and travel stocks crater
• Nasdaq: Drops 2.3% as geopolitical risk rattles tech valuations
• Airlines Grounded: Spirit Airlines shut down immediately after no buyer emerged, disrupting bookings and raising questions about remaining low-cost carriers' survival in a high-cost environment.
• Oil Crisis Unfolds: The Strait of Hormuz closure threatens to push crude prices sharply higher, reviving inflationary pressures that could force the Fed to reconsider its rate path.
• Yen Intervention: Japan deployed $34.5 billion to defend its currency—the largest forex operation in years—after the yen weakened past key levels.

• AI Defense Deals: Nvidia, Microsoft, and AWS expanded classified military AI contracts with the Pentagon, signaling accelerating government spending on artificial intelligence for national security.
Any escalation in U.S.-Iran hostilities could trigger an immediate spike in oil futures and a flight to safety across global markets.
